description: This test case evaluates the RAG system's robustness in retrieving a specific, rare, or hard-to-find fact (the 'needle') from a large context ('haystack').
question: Explain the design and functional advantages of the compliant dielectric interposer in semiconductor chip assemblies as described in US5148265, and how it compensates for thermal expansion and facilitates testing of high I/O count chips.

gold_context_sources.section_title: DETAILED DESCRIPTION OF THE PREFERRED EMBODIMENTS
gold_context_sources.evidence: A semiconductor chip assembly comprising: (a) a semiconductor chip having a front surface defining the top of the chip, said front surface including a central region and a peripheral region surrounding said central region, said chip having a plurality of peripheral contacts disposed in said peripheral region; (b) a sheetlike dielectric interposer overlying said central region of said chip front surface, said interposer having a first surface facing toward said chip and a second surface facing away from said chip, said interposer having outward edges disposed inwardly of said peripheral contacts; (c) a plurality of central terminals disposed on said interposer and overlying said central region of said chip front surface, said central terminals facing away from said chip and being exposed at said second surface of said interposer for interconnection to a substrate; and (d) a plurality of peripheral contact leads connecting at least some of said peripheral contacts and at least some of said central terminals, each said peripheral contact lead having a central terminal end overlying said interposer and connected to one of said central terminals and a contact end projecting outwardly beyond one of said edges of said interposer and connected to one of said peripheral contacts, whereby each said peripheral contact lead extends inwardly from one of said peripheral contacts to one of said central terminals on said interposer, said central terminals being movable with respect to said contacts so as to compensate for thermal expansion of said chip. The interposer includes a compliant layer of a low modulus material disposed beneath the central terminals allowing each central terminal to be displaced downwardly toward the chip upon deformation of the compliant layer, facilitating engagement of test probes with the terminals. Peripheral contact leads and bonding wires are flexible, and the interposer itself is flexible, allowing for movement to accommodate differential thermal expansion without applying substantial forces to junctions. The compliant layer includes holes interspersed with masses of low modulus material to provide backing and support for terminals and to allow lateral bulging during compression by test probes, enabling reliable simultaneous electrical testing of all terminals at full speed.

ground_truth_answer: The compliant dielectric interposer described in US5148265 provides significant design and functional advantages in semiconductor chip assemblies, especially for high I/O count chips. It is a sheetlike structure positioned over the central region of the chip's front surface, having central terminals on its outward-facing surface for connection to a substrate, and peripheral contact leads connecting chip peripheral contacts to these central terminals. The interposer includes a compliant layer made of a low modulus material (such as an elastomer) beneath the central terminals, which allows each terminal to be independently displaced downward toward the chip surface when engaged by test probes. This compliance accommodates manufacturing tolerances and differences in vertical position between terminals and probes, ensuring reliable electrical contact during testing. The compliant layer is engineered with holes interspersed with masses of low modulus material, providing structural support while allowing lateral bulging during compression, further enhancing contact reliability. Additionally, the peripheral contact leads and bonding wires are flexible, and the interposer itself is flexible, enabling the assembly to accommodate differential thermal expansion between the chip and substrate without imposing harmful mechanical stresses on the junctions between leads and contacts. This flexibility and compliance reduce stress during thermal cycling, improving reliability. The arrangement of central terminals at substantial center-to-center distances facilitates the use of standard surface mount technology for substrate bonding and allows simultaneous probing of all terminals at full speed with a rugged, reliable probe set. Overall, the compliant dielectric interposer improves electrical testing efficiency, mechanical robustness, and thermal stress accommodation in high-density semiconductor chip assemblies.
